skill.name: when-optimizing-prompts-use-prompt-optimization-analyzer
skill.description: Analyze prompts for token waste, vague instructions, trigger issues, and optimization opportunities before publishing or reuse.
skill.summary: Evaluates prompt quality and recommends clearer, shorter, more actionable prompt designs.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but the weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts are false positives caused by documentation text, Graphviz labels, and Markdown examples. The confirmed concern is documented external command execution through npx claude-flow@alpha and optional memory storage of prompt text, so publication is acceptable with a clear warning.

medium_findings.title: Documented External Command Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for the external command risk factor. The skill documentation includes fenced bash examples that run npx claude-flow@alpha hooks and memory commands. This appears workflow-related rather than malicious, but it can execute a remote package version and should be reviewed before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The npx commands are explicit and repeated in fenced bash examples. They are not hidden code, but npx can download and execute package code in the user environment.

medium_findings.title: Prompt Content May Be Stored in Workflow Memory
medium_findings.description: The process documentation shows examples that store original prompt text, tokens, metrics, recommendations, and optimized text in claude-flow memory. This is useful for analysis history, but users should avoid storing secrets or confidential prompt content without an approved retention policy.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly describes storing prompt text and metrics. I did not find evidence of network exfiltration, so the concern is data retention rather than confirmed data theft.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged locations are Graphviz settings, Markdown labels, prompt-analysis examples, and workflow command text. I did not find evidence of weak cryptographic algorithms such as MD5, SHA1, DES, or RC4 being used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the flagged lines shows documentation and labels, not cryptographic APIs or hashes used for authentication, encryption, or integrity.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines are post-task hook examples and task identifier placeholders, not commands that enumerate users, hosts, networks, processes, or environment details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context contains workflow hook arguments only. No reconnaissance commands or collection of system information were present at those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples that invoke npx claude-flow@alpha. Users should understand that npx may execute package code and may require network access depending on local cache state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is directly present in fenced examples. The risk depends on whether the user chooses to run those commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Workflow Memory Persistence
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples store prompt text and optimization results in memory keys. This can retain sensitive prompt content outside the immediate conversation context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly store prompt text-like values. No evidence showed transmission to an external endpoint.

content.value_statement: Long or vague prompts waste tokens and produce inconsistent AI results. This skill reviews prompt structure, clarity, triggers, and examples to suggest focused improvements.
actual_capabilities: Detects repeated phrases, filler words, and verbose prompt sections.
Flags vague instructions, undefined terms, conflicting requirements, and missing context.
Reviews trigger text for clarity, specificity, scope, and edge case coverage.
Creates before and after comparisons with estimated token savings.
Documents workflow hooks for storing metrics and optimization recommendations.
limitations: It provides diagnostic guidance, not a guaranteed measure of model performance.
Token counts and savings are estimates unless checked with the target model tokenizer.
The embedded scripts are illustrative and may require adaptation before automation.
It does not replace human review for sensitive, legal, medical, or policy prompts.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Skill for Publication
use_cases.description: Review a new skill prompt for unclear triggers, redundant sections, and missing context before marketplace submission.

use_cases.title: Reduce Cost in Repeated Workflows
use_cases.description: Find token-heavy instructions and replace them with shorter, clearer requirements for high-volume AI workflows.

use_cases.title: Improve Prompt Library Consistency
use_cases.description: Apply the same analysis framework across many prompts to standardize quality, structure, and maintenance practices.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Single Prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this prompt for token waste, vague instructions, and missing context. Return the top issues and concise fixes: [paste prompt]

prompt_templates.title: Optimize for Token Budget
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review when a prompt is too long.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this prompt against a target budget of [token limit]. Identify removable repetition, compressible sections, and expected savings while preserving intent.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Trigger Precision
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate these trigger instructions for false positives, false negatives, missing edge cases, and unclear scope. Suggest revised trigger language.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Prompt Library
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these prompts as a library. Identify shared anti-patterns, inconsistent formats, duplicated guidance, and a prioritized optimization plan.

output_examples.input: A code review prompt repeats broad advice such as be helpful, be thorough, and make sure the review is good.
output_examples.output: The skill would flag repeated guidance, vague quality terms, and missing review criteria, then propose a shorter checklist with measurable review categories.

output_examples.input: A skill trigger says to run when the user needs help with something related to docs.
output_examples.output: The skill would identify broad scope and unclear activation conditions, then suggest a trigger that names documentation tasks and exclusion cases.

output_examples.input: A long workflow prompt includes eight similar examples and repeated formatting rules.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend consolidating examples, referencing a shared format rule, and estimating token savings after the revision.

best_practices: Run the analysis before publishing prompts that other users or agents will reuse.
Verify estimated token savings with the tokenizer for the target model when precision matters.
Review memory storage examples before using the workflow with confidential prompt content.
anti_patterns: Treating token reduction as the only goal when clarity or safety requirements need detail.
Accepting automated suggestions without checking whether the optimized prompt preserves intent.
Running documented npx commands in sensitive environments without reviewing the package and data retention behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill rewrite prompts automatically?
faq.answer: It provides analysis and recommended revisions. Users should review and apply changes intentionally.

faq.question: Can it estimate token savings?
faq.answer: Yes. It can compare before and after versions and estimate token reduction from removed repetition and shorter wording.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with confidential prompts?
faq.answer: Use caution. Some workflow examples store prompt text in memory, so remove secrets or disable retention where needed.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation-focused, but it includes npx command examples and memory storage patterns that require user review.

faq.question: What prompts benefit most from this skill?
faq.answer: Reusable prompts, skill instructions, agent workflows, and long prompts with unclear triggers benefit most.
